summary: The plan has several contract-level contradictions that should be resolved before dispatch. Most critically, PRE_EVAL requires a run_id and state before either exists; the proposed fast-path manifest lacks required spec/plan artifacts and conflicts with its own single-job reviewer rule; and the post-diff safety check is not sequenced against the existing baseline-aware merge/removal lifecycle. The implementation streams are also not actually independent. These are architectural gaps rather than routine implementation details.

findings.claim: PRE_EVAL cannot emit the specified state or telemetry because run_id and state.json do not exist yet.
findings.evidence: Plan A3 produces the §2 pre-eval shape and appends a predicted event keyed by run_id at PRE_EVAL_DONE. The spec explicitly says the execution run does not exist pre-brainstorm. Existing state-machine.md defines state.json only inside docs/superpowers/execution/<run-id>/, and v-orchestrate.md creates the run-id only from a completed plan. Declined/full-pipeline requests therefore have no run_id with which to write or later join the predicted event.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a durable pre_eval_id independent of run_id. Key the pre-run JSON and predicted event by it, then append an explicit binding event when a run is created. Define how /v:status discovers unbound pre-evals. Alternatively, mint a real run envelope before PRE_EVAL and update every downstream contract accordingly.

findings.claim: The proposed fast-path manifest cannot satisfy its existing semantic contract, and its job cardinality is internally contradictory.
findings.evidence: scripts/compound-v-validate-manifest.py requires spec_path, plan_path, audits, acceptance_criteria, and jobs. The fast path skips brainstorming/planning, so no task produces the required spec or plan. Task 0 simultaneously requires a “single-job manifest” and a “fast-path reviewer job” tokenized with REVIEWER_TOKEN; including that reviewer makes the manifest contain at least two jobs. In addition, fast_path.localization and sentinel localization require a path, but A1 returns an in-memory object and A3 only embeds it in pre-eval JSON—no standalone localization artifact is produced.
findings.recommendation: Define a complete conditional fast-path schema: either produce committed minimal spec/plan artifacts or explicitly replace those fields for fast_path manifests; keep the sole implementer in jobs and model review as a dispatcher phase outside jobs, or change the cardinality rule; and either produce a localization artifact or reference the pre-eval record plus a defined JSON pointer.

findings.claim: The post-hoc reclassifier can miss real changes and may run after the evidence has already been merged or deleted.
findings.evidence: F2 specifies plain `git diff --numstat`, without the recorded baseline. That omits changes committed inside a worker worktree and omits untracked files. The existing dispatcher deliberately uses a pinned baseline and includes untracked/ignored files, then on PASS immediately applies the patch and removes the worktree (parallel-dispatcher.md Step 2b). The plan never places F2 before that merge/removal step or defines a durable patch artifact.
findings.recommendation: Run reclassification immediately after the scope gate and before any merge, commit, or worktree removal. Pass the same pinned baseline and authoritative changed-path set, compute numstat against that baseline, account explicitly for untracked files, and save a binary patch plus baseline SHA under the original run before transitioning. Define whether the escalated full pipeline starts from the clean baseline or from an intentionally applied patch.

findings.claim: The A–F workstreams are not parallel-safe despite being presented as independent streams after Task 0.
findings.evidence: A3 consumes B’s taxonomy, D’s churn output, and F1’s Tier-2 API. C1 consumes B. D1 consumes B’s taxonomy semantics, D2 invokes B’s validator, and C2 consumes F1’s precision reader. Task 0 is described as blocking B–F but not A, while the cross-cutting task assumes all A–F can complete independently. Workers created from the same post-Task-0 HEAD will not see concurrently authored dependencies.
findings.recommendation: Materialize an explicit DAG and dispatch waves: Task 0; then B1, A1, A2, F1 and any independent foundations; then D1/C1/F2; then D2/A3/C2; finally Z1. Add depends_on entries to the manifest rather than relying on interface prose.

findings.claim: A2 does not define an executable route for the claimed Sonnet classifier.
findings.evidence: compound-v-resolve-model.py requires a backend and only resolves a model name; it does not invoke a model. The Claude backend is an in-harness Task adapter, not callable from a standalone Python script. A2 nevertheless says the Python `classify()` function resolves light→Sonnet, while also mentioning a separate “codex path” without defining backend selection, command shape, read-only isolation, or output transport.
findings.recommendation: Choose one concrete contract: either make A2 a pure prompt/parser invoked by the parent through an in-harness light-tier Task, or define a specific headless backend CLI with read-only sandboxing, timeout supervision, and enum-schema parsing. Add a fake-CLI integration test that proves backend selection, timeout, closed stdin, token/output bounds, and unknown-on-error behavior.

findings.claim: The user-facing configuration and revocation surface is outside every task’s write scope.
findings.evidence: AC-10/AC-11 require an `off` kill-switch, remembered-category choices, and revocation via `/v:init`. Task 0 only creates pre-eval-config.md, and no task modifies commands/v-init.md. The existing v-init command owns the generated `.claude/compound-v.json` shape and currently knows nothing about pre_eval keys.
findings.recommendation: Add commands/v-init.md to an explicit task, define its ownership, and test initial defaults, malformed-value fallback, remembered-category display/revocation, and the hard `off` behavior.

findings.claim: The churn cache cannot be updated exactly from the schema given, and the normalization is not reproducible.
findings.evidence: D1 stores only normalized_churn, fix_revert_density, hot, and last_sha. An incremental `<last_sha>..HEAD` pass cannot recompute age- and size-normalized values exactly without prior numerators, denominators, timestamps, or size statistics. The plan also leaves the normalization formula, hot threshold, minimum history, format-commit rule, and non-ancestor/history-rewrite behavior unspecified.
findings.recommendation: Either rebuild the cache from full history each time or store sufficient raw aggregates and timestamps to support exact incremental updates. Specify formulas and thresholds as fixtures, and fail closed or rebuild when last_sha is not an ancestor of HEAD.

findings.claim: Tier-2 calibration rules and the displayed precision metric are underspecified and partly contradictory.
findings.evidence: F1’s selftest says full-pipeline-with-outcome rows count as calibration, while the iron invariant says legacy full-pipeline outcomes may only escalate and must never corroborate low. The plan also never defines the numerator and denominator for “precision,” which actual outcomes count as false positives, or how duplicate/missing predicted and actual events are handled.
findings.recommendation: Define separate cohorts: only accepted fast-path outcomes may support a healthy/lowering signal; full-pipeline outcomes may contribute only escalation evidence. Specify exact join, deduplication, precision, escalation-rate, and missing-event formulas in deterministic fixtures.

findings.claim: Pre-evaluation records are mutable by pathname and can silently corrupt prior audit trails.
findings.evidence: The path is derived only as `docs/superpowers/pre-eval/<request-slug>.json`. Repeating the same request slug overwrites the committed record that an older manifest may reference. The manifest also records only taxonomy_version while C1 intends to read the current taxonomy file, so taxonomy edits can change validation of a frozen run during resume.
findings.recommendation: Use a write-once identifier containing a timestamp/nonce or content hash and reject overwrites. Record and validate the taxonomy file’s commit SHA or content hash, and copy/pin the exact taxonomy snapshot into the run so resume does not depend on mutable project configuration.

findings.claim: The central fast-path test/review floor is described but not assigned a concrete implementation task.
findings.evidence: The spec requires configured tests, guarded language parse checks, fallback diff review, one combined Opus SPEC+QUALITY pass, and a recorded vacuous integration pass. C2 merely says “fast-path dispatch (single job) + escalation rejoin”; it does not define or test the binary-present/project-manifest-present ladder or combined-review invocation. Z1 is an end-to-end fixture task, not an owner for missing dispatcher behavior.
findings.recommendation: Expand C2 or add a dedicated fast-path runner task with exact test-floor commands, degrade/fail-closed rules, reviewer dispatch, recorded rationale, and state transitions. Add fake-tool fixtures for absent and failing language toolchains.

findings.claim: The structural escalation safety net is too vague to be falsifiable across supported files.
findings.evidence: F2 promises a “bounded structural pass for new control-flow/signature” but defines no supported languages, parsing strategy, behavior on parse failure, handling for deleted/renamed/binary files, or ambiguity rule. Its tests cover only one changed function signature and one CSS example, despite this analyzer being the final safety net after ceremony is skipped.
findings.recommendation: Declare supported analyzers and exact triggers. Unsupported syntax, parse failure, binary changes, or ambiguous structural detection should escalate. Add fixtures for every supported language plus deletions, renames, untracked files, binary files, parse errors, and worker-committed changes.

findings.claim: The taxonomy contract is not precise enough for its four independent consumers to agree.
findings.evidence: B1 defines content_patterns with only `{match, kind, impact_band}`. It does not say whether match is literal, regex, or glob; whether it is case-sensitive; whether it scans whole files or changed lines; or how invalid/expensive patterns behave. A1, A3, C1, D1, and F2 are expected to interpret this independently.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit pattern type, case behavior, scan scope, normalization, and validation limits to the schema, and provide a single shared loader/matcher module or conformance fixture suite used by every consumer.

findings.claim: The declared file partition is not complete enough to materialize a disjoint manifest.
findings.evidence: E1’s Files list omits the fixture ADR that Step 3 creates. Z1 allows either a new `tests/` file or edits to embedded selftests “where the repo convention places it,” which could reopen files owned by A–F. This contradicts the plan’s claim that write surfaces are locked and disjoint.
findings.recommendation: Name the exact ADR fixture and exact integration-test files before manifest generation. If Z1 must edit A–F scripts, move those edits into their owning tasks or make Z1 a declared serial integration owner after removing those paths from earlier write scopes.

blind_spots_checked: Expanded the stated workstream file lists and checked Task 0 versus Workstream C for direct write overlap; their explicitly listed edits can be serially separated.
Checked existing manifest required fields, reviewer detection, empty reviewer write scopes, and single-job implications.
Checked current state/run-id creation, status/resume storage, and manifest immutability contracts.
Checked the current scope-gate baseline semantics and dispatcher merge/worktree-removal order.
Checked model-tier resolution and the difference between in-harness Claude Task dispatch and headless external CLIs.
Checked `/v:init` ownership of `.claude/compound-v.json` and remembered-choice revocation coverage.
Checked append-only telemetry joins, counterfactual calibration, precision definitions, and record durability.
Checked taxonomy consumers, churn incrementality, post-diff handling of committed/untracked changes, and resume determinism.
Checked model policy: reviewers remain deep/Opus and no Haiku route is proposed; the unresolved issue is the executable T3 backend path.
Checked acceptance coverage for the test floor, combined review, onboarding gate, ADR indexing, and end-to-end escalation.
